1: A shadow: Solomon's marriage alliance with Pharaoh king of Egypt violates the Deuteronomic prohibition (Deuteronomy 17:17; cf. Nehemiah 13:26) and foreshadows the spiritual compromise that will end his reign. That he brings Pharaoh's daughter into the city of David — the holy city — signals the entanglement of covenant kingship with foreign power. The greater Son of David will not compromise covenant loyalty; he comes not to make treaties with the powers of this age but to defeat them (Colossians 2:15).
2: A theme: the absence of a house for YHWH's name explains why the people still sacrificed at the high places. The longing for the temple runs through 1-2 Kings as a longing for the proper dwelling of God among his people. The NT presents Christ as the true fulfillment of that longing: 'in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily' (Colossians 2:9) and 'the Word tabernacled among us' (John 1:14). Every high-place substitute for genuine divine presence anticipates the NT's verdict that only in Christ is the presence of God with his people made full and permanent.
3: A revelation of God: Solomon's love for YHWH and his walk in his father David's statutes establishes the standard by which his later apostasy will be measured. The qualification — 'except that he offered sacrifices at the high places' — is the seed of future failure. The NT uses this trajectory to illustrate that partial obedience is not covenant faithfulness; the law requires perfect obedience (Galatians 3:10), which only Christ fulfills on behalf of his people (Romans 8:3-4).
4: A theme: Solomon's thousand burnt offerings at Gibeon represent extravagant piety, a devotion that YHWH honors with the dream theophany. The 'most prominent high place' will soon be superseded by the Jerusalem temple, just as every earthly structure for worship is superseded by Christ himself as the meeting place of God and humanity (John 4:21-24; Hebrews 9:11-12). Lavish worship offered in good faith toward YHWH is the correct instinct; Christ fulfills and redirects it to himself.
5: A revelation of God: the Gibeon dream — 'Ask whatever you wish and I will give you' — is one of the OT's clearest pictures of divine grace reaching toward the king before he has done anything. God approaches Solomon; God initiates the gift. The NT makes this structure explicit in Christ: 'You did not choose me, but I chose you' (John 15:16); 'while we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8). God's gracious approach to Solomon in a dream is a shadow of the incarnation itself — God coming near to his people with an open hand.
6: A theme: Solomon appeals to YHWH's steadfast love (hesed) toward David — the covenant-loyalty that is the ground of all the blessings he is about to receive. This covenant structure anticipates the NT's argument in Romans: the blessings of salvation come not through individual merit but through God's covenant faithfulness, now revealed through Christ who is the 'yes' to all God's promises (2 Corinthians 1:20). Solomon stands in the stream of grace flowing to him because of his father; believers stand in the stream of grace flowing to them because of Christ.
7: A theme: Solomon's confession of inadequacy — 'I am only a young man; I do not know how to go out or come in' — is the posture that makes him capable of asking for wisdom rather than power. The NT presents Christ as the one who, though equal with God, 'did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped' but emptied himself (Philippians 2:6-7). Solomon's humility is a shadow; Christ's kenosis is the reality. Both receive divine exaltation as a result (1 Kings 3:13; Philippians 2:9-11).
8: A theme: 'Your servant stands in the midst of your chosen people, a great people too numerous to be counted.' The Abrahamic promise of a people as numerous as the sand (Genesis 22:17) is already partially realized in Solomon's Israel. The greater fulfillment is the church drawn from all nations: 'a great multitude that no one could count, from every nation, tribe, people and language' (Revelation 7:9). Solomon's awe at the size of the people he is called to lead points toward the scope of Christ's kingdom that no earthly monarch can contain.
9: A type: Solomon's request for 'an understanding mind to govern your people, that I may discern between good and evil' is a request for wisdom rather than power. Jesus identifies himself with this exact theme: 'something greater than Solomon is here' (Matthew 12:42). Paul identifies Christ as 'the wisdom of God' (1 Corinthians 1:24) and says that 'in him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' (Colossians 2:3). Solomon's request is the OT's most explicit royal petition for wisdom; Christ is its fulfillment. What Solomon asked for, Christ is — not as a gift received but as the eternal nature of the Son of God.
10: A revelation of God: 'It pleased the Lord that Solomon had asked for this.' YHWH's delight in Solomon's request rather than requests for wealth, power, or vengeance reveals God's own priorities: wisdom and justice for the people over personal advancement. This delight anticipates the Father's words at Jesus's baptism — 'This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased' (Matthew 3:17) — spoken not after a list of royal achievements but after an act of covenant identification (baptism with sinners). The Father delights in the Son who comes not to be served but to serve (Matthew 20:28).
11: A revelation of God: God's comparison of Solomon's request with possible requests for long life, riches, or enemies' deaths reveals the theological hierarchy of values in the covenant: justice for the people ranks above personal benefit. This hierarchy is embodied in Christ, who 'did not please himself' (Romans 15:3) and gave himself for others rather than seeking his own preservation. God's response to Solomon reveals that the king who forgets himself in service to others is the king who most fully reflects God's own character.
12: A type: 'I give you a wise and discerning mind, so that no one like you has existed before and no one like you will arise after you.' This superlative — unprecedented wisdom — is immediately relativized by Jesus's 'something greater than Solomon is here' (Matthew 12:42). Solomon's incomparable wisdom is real but partial; it is human wisdom supernaturally gifted. Christ's wisdom is divine wisdom incarnate. The NT contrast is not Solomon versus nothing, but Solomon's shadow versus Christ's reality (Colossians 2:17).
13: A theme: 'I also give you what you did not ask for — both wealth and honor.' The principle that seeking first the kingdom produces additional gifts appears in its seed form here: Solomon sought wisdom for the people's sake and received wisdom plus all the goods he did not seek. Jesus teaches the same structure: 'Seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all these things will be added to you' (Matthew 6:33). The Solomonic narrative is the narrative example of the teaching Jesus will give.
14: A theme: 'And if you walk in my ways... I will give you long life.' The conditional promise of long life for covenant obedience is the Deuteronomic framework throughout 1 Kings. Solomon does not ultimately walk in YHWH's ways, and dies; but Christ walks in perfect covenant obedience and dies anyway — bearing the curse of others' disobedience (Galatians 3:13). In Christ, the conditionality is resolved: he fulfilled the condition perfectly, so the blessing flows to all who are in him, not on the basis of their own walk but his.
15: A theme: Solomon awakes and stands before the ark of the covenant, offering burnt offerings and peace offerings. The ark — the seat of YHWH's presence between the cherubim — is the OT meeting point between God and his people. Solomon's instinct after the divine encounter is to return to the place of divine presence. The ark's role as the earthly localization of YHWH's presence gives way, in the NT, to the ascended Christ who is the presence of God at the Father's right hand (Hebrews 9:24), our advocate and high priest before the Father.
16: A theme: the transition from the dream theophany to a realistic legal dispute — two prostitutes and a dead child — is striking. The wisdom God gives Solomon is not otherworldly but practical: it operates in the messiest human situations. Christ's wisdom similarly engages the actual messy world: tax collectors, adulterous women, lepers, demon-possessed men. The 'wisdom from above' (James 3:17) is not speculative but ethical and judicial, operating precisely where human judgment fails.
17: A theme: the first woman's claim and the narrative of the overlapping births establishes the legal impossibility of the case: there are no witnesses, and both mothers claim the living child. This is the kind of judicial impasse that cannot be resolved by normal evidence — only by wisdom that can read what witnesses cannot see. Christ's wisdom similarly penetrates to the heart: 'he knew what was in man' (John 2:25) and answers questions from the heart's motive rather than its surface claim (Matthew 22:15-22).
18: A theme: 'We were alone — there was no one else with us in the house.' The absence of witnesses is the theological problem: justice normally depends on testimony. The Mosaic law requires two or three witnesses (Deuteronomy 17:6). Solomon's wisdom will provide what witnesses cannot. The NT parallel is Christ's authority to judge without the normal evidential requirement: 'He who sent me is with me ... and I judge truly' (John 8:16). Divine wisdom supplements human evidence.
19: A theme: the woman's dead son and her midnight substitution with the living child sets up the central question of the narrative: which child is alive? The life-death contrast in this story resonates with the NT's central theme: the death of one (the child overlaid) and the life of another, with a mother's anguish at the center. That the true mother prefers her child live with another rather than die is a shadow of Christ's willingness to give his life rather than see others perish (John 3:16).
20: A theme: 'She placed her dead son at my breast and took my living child.' The substitution — a dead child for a living one — echoes the structure of substitutionary sacrifice without being a type: here the substitution is criminal. But it frames the question that drives the narrative: whose child is the living one? In the gospel, Christ takes the death of sinners (substitution) so that the living Child — the Son — might be known as theirs by adoption (Romans 8:15-17). The motif of life-death substitution pervades both stories.
21: A theme: the mother's careful morning examination of the child — 'it was not the son I bore' — is the moment of recognition that drives her to the king. Only the king can render justice when human examination has reached its limit. This reaches toward Christ as the final judge to whom the wronged bring their case; the NT presents Christ as the one who 'will bring to light the things now hidden in darkness and will disclose the purposes of the heart' (1 Corinthians 4:5).
22: A theme: the two women's contradictory claims before the king — each insisting the living child is hers — produce a legal deadlock that normal judicial procedure cannot resolve. Solomon's genius is to create a situation where the true mother's love will betray her. Christ's wisdom similarly reframes impossible situations: when Pharisees try to trap him between Rome and God (Matthew 22:15-22), his answer bypasses the trap entirely and answers at a deeper level.
23: A theme: Solomon restates the dispute with precision: two claims, each the mirror image of the other. The king's clarity about the structure of the problem is the first move in resolving it. Wisdom is first diagnostic before it is prescriptive. Christ's wisdom similarly diagnoses before prescribing: he names the real question behind the surface question in every controversy, exposing what his opponents are actually asking (Mark 12:13-17).
24: A theme: 'Bring me a sword.' The sword command is deliberately ambiguous — it could be the king enacting an atrocity or revealing the truth. It is wisdom's instrument of discernment: the order that only the true mother cannot accept. Christ uses similarly arresting, even shocking language to surface the real position of hearts: 'Do you think I have come to bring peace on earth? No, but rather division' (Luke 12:51). Wisdom sometimes shocks in order to reveal.
25: A shadow: 'Cut the living child in two.' The impossible command — which would destroy what both women claim to want — is designed to produce the response of authentic love. No true mother would accept half a living child over a whole dead one. This shadow points toward Gethsemane: Christ does not divide himself, does not offer half — he gives the whole of himself (John 10:11, 17-18). The sword judgment is the crucible that reveals who truly loves and who only claims to.
26: A type: 'The woman whose son was alive cried out to the king — her heart ached for her son — Please, my lord, give her the living child, and do not kill him.' The true mother's love is revealed precisely when she surrenders her claim rather than see the child die. This is the shape of genuine love in the biblical narrative: Abraham willing to give up Isaac (Genesis 22:12), the father running to the prodigal (Luke 15:20), Christ giving his life for others (John 15:13). Love that would rather let go than see the beloved destroyed is the NT's definition of agape.
27: A type: 'Give the living child to the first woman, and do not kill him. She is his mother.' Solomon's verdict returns the child to his true mother through wisdom that sees what no witness saw. A type of Christ's wisdom: the one who truly belongs to Christ is returned to him not through human force but through a wisdom that reads the heart. The NT's application is the image of Christ knowing his own: 'My sheep hear my voice, and I know them' (John 10:27). The judge who can see through pretense and return the living to their true owner is the shadow; Christ is the substance.
28: A shadow: all Israel heard the judgment and stood in awe of Solomon, because they saw that God's wisdom was in him. The awe of the people at Solomon's wisdom is the shadow of the awe at Christ's wisdom: the crowds 'were astonished at his teaching, for he taught them as one who had authority' (Matthew 7:28-29). What Israel glimpses in Solomon's judgment — divine wisdom operating through a human king — is the reality in Christ, who is not merely an instrument of divine wisdom but is wisdom itself (1 Corinthians 1:24, 30).
